content:
A new post hit r/selfhosted (and got cross-posted to r/LocalLLaMA):

Title: "exo — Multi-agent simulation engine that scans your hardware
+ services first, generates a config tailored to YOUR machine.
Local-first, Apache 2.0."

Body (from the poster): "Tired of LLM agent frameworks that assume
you'll wire your own everything. Built a thing that probes localhost
for Ollama / Qdrant / Neo4j / Postgres / Claude OAuth / Ollama Cloud,
then writes a docker-compose + YAML config that uses what you have.
Optional databases are profile-gated — `docker compose up` starts
just the runner; `docker compose --profile graph up` adds Neo4j only
when you need it. Sales-call rehearsal example + Mirofish-recreate
example in the repo with full transcripts. Apache 2.0."

You clicked through. You read the README, the WHY, and the Quickstart.
You scanned the example transcript. Now you're commenting on Reddit.

This is *exactly* the kind of project I love to see. Scans your stack, writes the compose file, and respects existing topology? Big yes. Already eyeing that `--profile` flag usage for optional DBs — keeps my homelab lean and mean. YAML config + deterministic architect CLI? Sign me up. Stars incoming once I test it against my 3-node Proxmox setup.

Apache 2.0 is a solid permissive choice for something like this — gives users real freedom to self-host without copyleft pressure. The local-first design aligns well with /r/selfhosted values. I'm curious how the deterministic CLI holds up across complex domains, but the examples show promise. No hardcoded API keys or cloud lock-in is a big plus.

The real test will be whether the memory-tier abstraction actually works as advertised. If I can run a full simulation against my local Neo4j without touching vendor infrastructure, that's genuinely useful. Will try it this weekend.

Solid project ethos — no cloud accounts, hardware-aware config, and Apache 2.0. I respect that. Already thinking about how this fits into my 4-node Proxmox setup with Ceph backing it. The `--profile` flags for optional DBs are smart; I don't need Qdrant/Neo4j unless I'm modeling something complex.

YAML-first design means I can version-control my simulations — big plus for reproducibility. Curious if the LLM router handles local llama-swap cleanly; I run mixed models depending on workload.

Will test with a sales-call scenario first. Stars pending successful transcript generation without hitting Ollama Cloud.

This looks *exactly* like the kind of tool I wish I had six months ago when I was fumbling through my first LXC containers. The part that sold me was:

> `docker compose up` starts just the runner; `docker compose --profile graph up` adds Neo4j only when you need it.

That’s the kind of clear docs and predictable behavior that makes me feel capable, not confused. I'm running a NUC with Proxmox and three LXCs right now — this could slot right into my setup without breaking things.

I also love that it doesn’t assume I have cloud keys or want to send data out. Big plus for local-first design.

Will try the sales-pipeline template this weekend and report back.

This looks promising but I'm skeptical about local LLM routing. Does the `actor.model` field actually work with llama.cpp backends or is it hardcoded to Ollama only? I run qwen2.5:14b on my 4090 via llama.cpp and need confirmation that's a supported path before diving in.

Also, what's the memory footprint like for these simulations? If I'm running 8 actors with local models, I don't want to OOM my GPU. The examples show transcript output but not VRAM usage.

This is promising — genuinely local execution and Apache 2.0 are table stakes I rarely see met. The deterministic CLI and YAML config align with my ethos, and the `--profile` flags for optional DBs show real architectural discipline.

However, I'm scrutinizing the LLM router section. If it defaults to Ollama Cloud without explicit local flags, that's a hard pass. Will grep through the source for any hardcoded remote endpoints before starring.

Good start. Needs rigorous air-gap validation.

That's exactly right — the `--profile` flags are *chef's kiss*. I run a lean homelab with 3 Proxmox nodes and no room for bloat. Having Qdrant/Neo4j/Postgres opt-in via profile instead of always-on is huge. Means I can drop this into my existing setup with one compose file, no restructuring.

Scanned the `docker-compose.yml` and it's clean — healthchecks, restart policies, and named volumes where expected. The `exo doctor` hardware scan before config gen is smart too. Stars incoming once I verify it doesn't tangle with my existing Traefik setup.

The Apache 2.0 license gives users real freedom to self-host without copyleft pressure, which is exactly what I look for in tools that claim to be "local-first." The deterministic CLI and YAML-first config are also big wins for reproducibility and version control. 

I'm particularly impressed by the memory-tier abstraction — if it works as advertised, this could be genuinely useful for modeling complex domains without vendor lock-in. The fact that the default `docker compose up` starts only the runner is a sign of good architectural discipline.

Still, I'd like to see how it handles more complex simulations beyond the sales-pipeline example. Does anyone have experience running this with a full memory stack (Qdrant + Neo4j + Postgres) locally? That's where the real test of the abstraction will come.

Solid first impression. I like that it doesn't force cloud accounts — aligns with my local-first homelab ethos. The `--profile` flags for optional DBs are smart; keeps my Proxmox cluster from spinning unnecessary resources. 

My main question: does the LLM router actually handle local llama-swap cleanly? I run mixed models depending on workload. If I can route some actors to frontier (Claude OAuth) and others to local (llama-swap/qwen2.5), that’s a big plus. 

Will test with sales-call scenario first. Stars pending successful transcript generation without hitting Ollama Cloud.

That sales-pipeline example looks super approachable — exactly what I need to get comfortable with the tool. I'm already thinking about spinning up a test LXC just to isolate it from my other containers. The fact that I can run the whole thing locally without any API keys is a huge relief; I don't want to mess with cloud credentials yet.

Also, big plus for keeping the DBs optional. My NUC doesn't have RAM to spare, so running lean by default feels *so* right.

The `actor.model` field supports `local-ollama/<model>` routing, which works with llama.cpp backends if you're running Ollama locally. I swapped in `local-ollama/qwen2.5:14b` on my 4090 and it respected the model without OOM — VRAM sat around 7.2GB during 8-agent runs. Not bad.

Still poking at the llama-swap integration though; early tests show model load failures unless I manually symlink the GGUFs into Ollama's blobs dir. Could be my setup.

The project checks a lot of my boxes: Apache 2.0, deterministic config, and local execution are all wins. I'm particularly glad to see no hardcoded cloud endpoints in the `LLM router` section of the README — that alone separates it from half the tools on GitHub.

That said, I'm not fully sold until I verify the `local-ollama` model routing doesn't phone home under stress. The `--profile` flags for databases are excellent; zero dependencies unless explicitly enabled is how every self-hosted tool should work.

I'll be testing this in an air-gapped VM with WireGuard soon. If it respects true offline mode and doesn't fall back to cloud APIs silently, it gets a star from me.

Just spun up a test run on my Proxmox cluster — clean install, no snags. The `--profile` flags work *exactly* as advertised, and the YAML config is trivial to version-control. No Traefik conflicts, no unexpected port binds. 

What sold me was the `exo doctor` output actually matching my hardware + service scan. Real plug-and-play experience. Stars earned.

The deterministic architect CLI and YAML-first approach are table stakes for anything claiming to be self-hostable. Apache 2.0 is acceptable here — no viral copyleft forcing SaaS forks back to OSS, but at least it's not MIT-with-hidden-trust-breaker clauses like some "open source" tools.

What I want to see is whether the memory-tier abstraction actually works when all three DBs are spun up via `--profile all`. If the graph + vector + structured layers stay isolated and performant without forcing me into vendor-specific query patterns, that's where the real value lands. Most "multi-agent" tools collapse into spaghetti when you add memory indirection.

Still eyeing the license — Apache 2.0 keeps commercial forks honest without forcing them OSS. That's rarer than you'd think in agent-land.

Just finished reviewing the README and example transcripts — this hits close to home for my Proxmox+Ceph setup. The `--profile` flags for optional DBs are smart; keeps my cluster lean when running simple simulations.

My main question from the docs: does the LLM router actually handle local llama-swap cleanly? I run mixed models depending on workload. If I can route some actors to frontier (Claude OAuth) and others to local (llama-swap/qwen2.5), that's a big plus.

Will test with sales-call scenario first. Stars pending successful transcript generation without hitting Ollama Cloud.

This is exactly the kind of project that makes me excited to keep building my homelab skills. The clear documentation and predictable behavior really make me feel like I can jump in without breaking everything. I'm already planning to test the sales-pipeline template this weekend on my NUC with Proxmox. The fact that it runs locally and keeps databases optional is perfect for my resource-limited setup. Can't wait to see how it performs!

Poked around the codebase — the `local-ollama/<model>` routing does work with llama.cpp backends if you're running Ollama locally. I swapped in `local-ollama/qwen2.5:14b` on my 4090 and it stayed under 8GB VRAM during 8-agent runs. The model loading respects llama.cpp flags like `--n-gpu-layers` if you set them in Ollama's Modelfile.

Still testing llama-swap integration; early results show model load failures unless I manually symlink GGUFs into Ollama's blobs dir. Could be my setup, but worth noting for tinkerers who swap models frequently.

The README's LLM router section specifically mentions `local-ollama/<model>` as a supported path, which is encouraging. If the implementation truly isolates local inference without fallbacks to cloud APIs, this could be a rare tool that respects air-gapped workflows. Will grep the source for any outbound calls before starring — mandatory for any self-respecting privacy setup.

Pulled the repo and ran it on my Proxmox cluster — zero drama. The `--profile` flags are *exactly* what I want: lean by default, scale up when needed. No tangled dependencies, no forced restructuring of my existing topology.

The YAML config is trivial to version-control, and the deterministic architect CLI actually works as advertised. I pointed it at my hardware with `exo doctor`, and the generated compose file respected every service flag.

Big plus: no Traefik conflicts, no unexpected port binds. Just clean, predictable Docker behavior. Stars earned. Already planning to port my sales-call rehearsal from Mirofish to this.

The deterministic architect CLI and YAML-first approach are promising — Apache 2.0 doesn't force SaaS forks back to OSS, but at least it's not MIT-with-hidden-trust-breaker clauses. I'm curious how the memory-tier abstraction handles full-stack runs (`--profile all`) without collapsing into vendor-specific query spaghetti. Real multi-agent simulations need robust memory indirection that scales without forcing cloud dependencies.

Still eyeing the license — Apache 2.0 keeps commercial forks honest without forcing them OSS. That's rarer than you'd think in agent-land.
